result.markdown:
---
title: "Generalization"
subtitle: "INFO 521 · Module 2 · Lecture A — Evaluation & Cross-Validation"
author: "Greg Chism"
institute: "School of Information · University of Arizona"
date: last-modified
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"
---


## Learning outcomes {.smaller}

By the end of Lecture A you should be able to:

1. **Distinguish training error from generalization error.**
2. **Diagnose overfitting** from the gap between them.
3. **Run $k$-fold cross-validation.**
4. **Select a model or hyperparameter** by cross-validation.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: the whole lecture is "how well will this do on data it hasn't
seen?" — and how to estimate that honestly.
:::

## Bridge from Module 1 {.smaller}

In Lecture 1B we saw **basis functions** let us make the model *arbitrarily
flexible* — swap $\mathbf{x}\to\boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x})$, raise the degree.

- Flexibility **buys fit**: a high-degree curve can pass through every point.
- It also risks **memorizing** the sample instead of learning the pattern.

[How much flexibility is *right*? That question is this whole module.]{style="color: var(--accent)"}

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: pick up the explicit 1B hook — the degree-9 wiggle. Today we make
"too flexible" measurable.
:::

## Overfitting, made visible

<!-- ADDED: not in Clay source — illustrative nonlinear signal (see setup), since
     real SBP–age is near-linear and shows no underfitting region. -->

::: {#cell-fig-overfit .cell execution_count=2}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![Illustrative signal, 30 points, three complexities: degree 1 (underfit), 3, 9 (overfit).](m2a-evaluation-cv_files/figure-revealjs/fig-overfit-output-1.svg){#fig-overfit fig-alt='Scatter of about 30 points from an illustrative nonlinear signal on the NHANES age grid, with three polynomial fits: a degree-1 line that misses the curvature (underfit), a degree-3 curve that tracks it, and a wiggly degree-9 curve that chases individual points (overfit).'}
:::
:::


## The trap: training error always drops {.smaller}

Make the model more flexible and the **training error only ever goes down** — a
degree-$k$ polynomial can interpolate $k{+}1$ points *exactly* ($\mathcal{L}=0$).

- Low training error is **easy to buy** with complexity.
- It says nothing about new patients.

[**Training error is not generalization error.**]{style="color: var(--accent)"}

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: this is the core misconception to break. Zero training loss can mean
a perfect model or a memorized one — training error can't tell you which.
:::

## Hold out a test set {.smaller}

Estimate generalization by scoring on data the model **never saw during fitting**:

- **Split** the patients into a **training** set (fit $\hat{\mathbf{w}}$) and a
  **test** set (score only).
- Test error is an honest estimate of error on *new* patients.

When we also tune a knob, a third **validation** ("dev") set does the tuning, so
the test set stays untouched until the very end.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: (Clay 06/07 — the dev-set slide). The test set is sacred: touch it
once, at the end. Tuning on it silently overfits the estimate.
:::

## Training vs. test vs. complexity

<!-- ADDED: not in Clay source — illustrative nonlinear signal (see setup), so the
     full bias-variance U is visible; real SBP–age is near-linear. -->

::: {#cell-fig-train-test .cell execution_count=3}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![The central picture (illustrative signal, median over 30 training resamples): training error ↓; test error U-shaped.](m2a-evaluation-cv_files/figure-revealjs/fig-train-test-output-1.svg){#fig-train-test fig-alt='Training and test mean-squared error versus polynomial degree for the illustrative signal. Training error (blue circles) falls monotonically as degree rises; test error (vermillion squares) is U-shaped — high at low degree (underfit), lowest in the middle, rising again at high degree (overfit).'}
:::
:::


## The generalization gap {.smaller}

The distance between the two curves diagnoses the model:

- **Overfit** — low training error, high test error (the gap is wide, right side).
- **Underfit** — both high (left side); the model is too rigid to fit the signal.
- **Just right** — the bottom of the test-error U.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: name the regions on the previous figure. The generalization gap =
test − train; it widens as complexity outruns the data.
:::

## Why cross-validation? {.smaller}

A single train/test split has two problems, sharper at this sample size:

- it **wastes data** — every held-out patient is one you didn't train on;
- it is **high-variance** — a different split gives a different test error.

**Cross-validation** reuses every patient for both roles, in turn.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: (Clay 06). Motivate the reuse: we want a stable estimate and can't
afford to sacrifice a big test set.
:::

## $k$-fold cross-validation {.smaller}

Partition into $k$ folds; each fold takes a turn as the **validation** set while
the other $k{-}1$ **train**; average the $k$ scores.

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 820 250" width="100%" style="max-height:40vh; color: var(--text); font-family: inherit;"
     role="img" aria-label="Five-fold cross-validation schematic: five rows, each partitioned into five equal blocks. In row i the i-th block is the validation fold (accent color) and the other four are training blocks; the validation block rotates down the diagonal across rows.">
  <text x="10" y="20" fill="currentColor" stroke="none" font-size="13">5-fold: the validation block (accent) rotates across rounds</text>
  <!-- rows -->
  <g font-size="12">
    <!-- generated: 5 rows × 5 cells -->
    <!-- row template drawn below -->
  </g>
  <g stroke="var(--surface)" stroke-width="2">
    <!-- Row 0 --> <g transform="translate(60,40)">
      <rect x="0"   y="0" width="140" height="28" style="fill: var(--accent);"/><rect x="140" y="0" width="140" height="28" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.12"/><rect x="280" y="0" width="140" height="28" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.12"/><rect x="420" y="0" width="140" height="28" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.12"/><rect x="560" y="0" width="140" height="28" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.12"/>
      <text x="720" y="19" fill="currentColor" stroke="none">round 1</text></g>
    <!-- Row 1 --> <g transform="translate(60,74)">
      <rect x="0"   y="0" width="140" height="28" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.12"/><rect x="140" y="0" width="140" height="28" style="fill: var(--accent);"/><rect x="280" y="0" width="140" height="28" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.12"/><rect x="420" y="0" width="140" height="28" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.12"/><rect x="560" y="0" width="140" height="28" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.12"/>
      <text x="720" y="19" fill="currentColor" stroke="none">round 2</text></g>
    <!-- Row 2 --> <g transform="translate(60,108)">
      <rect x="0"   y="0" width="140" height="28" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.12"/><rect x="140" y="0" width="140" height="28" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.12"/><rect x="280" y="0" width="140" height="28" style="fill: var(--accent);"/><rect x="420" y="0" width="140" height="28" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.12"/><rect x="560" y="0" width="140" height="28" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.12"/>
      <text x="720" y="19" fill="currentColor" stroke="none">round 3</text></g>
    <!-- Row 3 --> <g transform="translate(60,142)">
      <rect x="0"   y="0" width="140" height="28" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.12"/><rect x="140" y="0" width="140" height="28" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.12"/><rect x="280" y="0" width="140" height="28" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.12"/><rect x="420" y="0" width="140" height="28" style="fill: var(--accent);"/><rect x="560" y="0" width="140" height="28" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.12"/>
      <text x="720" y="19" fill="currentColor" stroke="none">round 4</text></g>
    <!-- Row 4 --> <g transform="translate(60,176)">
      <rect x="0"   y="0" width="140" height="28" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.12"/><rect x="140" y="0" width="140" height="28" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.12"/><rect x="280" y="0" width="140" height="28" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.12"/><rect x="420" y="0" width="140" height="28" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.12"/><rect x="560" y="0" width="140" height="28" style="fill: var(--accent);"/>
      <text x="720" y="19" fill="currentColor" stroke="none">round 5</text></g>
  </g>
  <!-- legend -->
  <g transform="translate(60,214)" font-size="12">
    <rect x="0" y="0" width="20" height="14" style="fill: var(--accent);"/>
    <text x="26" y="12" fill="currentColor" stroke="none">validation fold</text>
    <rect x="150" y="0" width="20" height="14" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.12"/>
    <text x="176" y="12" fill="currentColor" stroke="none">training folds</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: every patient validates exactly once and trains $k{-}1$ times. The
CV error is the average of the five round scores.
:::

## Cross-validation error curve

<!-- ADDED: not in Clay source — illustrative nonlinear signal (see setup). -->

::: {#cell-fig-cv .cell execution_count=4}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![5-fold CV error vs. degree (illustrative signal; band = ±1 SD across folds); minimum marks the CV choice.](m2a-evaluation-cv_files/figure-revealjs/fig-cv-output-1.svg){#fig-cv fig-alt='Five-fold cross-validation error versus polynomial degree for the illustrative signal, drawn as a green line with a shaded band showing the spread across folds. The curve is U-shaped with its minimum at a low-to-moderate degree; the band reflects fold-to-fold variability.'}
:::
:::


## Model selection by CV {.smaller}

**Pick the complexity that minimizes CV error.** Then refit that model on *all* the
training data and report on the held-out test set.

- CV is a *selection* tool — it chooses the knob.
- The final, untouched test set is what you *report*.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: selection vs reporting. CV picks the degree/λ; the test set gives the
number you trust. Don't let them be the same data.
:::

## The pitfall: leakage {.smaller}

Cross-validation only tells the truth if the folds are **honestly separated**:

- **Preprocess inside each fold.** Standardize (center/scale) using the *training*
  fold's statistics, then apply to the validation fold — never on the pooled data.
- **Never tune on the test set.** Every peek leaks information and inflates the
  estimate.

[Same discipline as the Module 1 feature-leakage rule — the answer must not sneak
into the inputs.]{style="color: var(--accent)"}

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: (Clay 07 — leakage is a major theme there). Fitting the scaler on
all data before CV is the classic silent leak; it makes CV look better than it is.
:::

## Recap & bridge {.smaller}

- **Training error** falls with complexity; **test/CV error** is U-shaped.
- **$k$-fold CV** gives a stable estimate and picks the knob.
- **Leakage** breaks the estimate — separate folds honestly.

[CV tells us **where** we are on the U. Next (Lecture B): **why** the U exists —
the bias-variance decomposition — and how to **move along it** with
regularization.]{style="color: var(--accent)"}

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: 2A is diagnosis; 2B is mechanism + control. The U-curve is the hinge
between them.
:::
